problem_summary: 2015 MCM Problem B (discrete), "Hidden in Plane Sight: Finding Lost Airplanes Before Time Runs Out" — design a search strategy to locate a commercial airplane lost over open ocean (motivated by MH370) in minimum time. The team frames it as a two-part problem: (1) WHERE to look — derive a bounded rectangular search area and a probability-of-containment distribution for the crash site; and (2) HOW to look — design and benchmark search-plane flight paths that minimize expected time-to-detection. They escalate from an idealized (perfect-detection) model to a realistic model with imperfect radar detection, and tie success to a human-survival (hypothermia) deadline.
models_used: Geometric search-area construction: glide range r = κ·α (glide ratio × altitude) swept along a flight segment L = v·τ (cruise speed × 30-min ATC-comms gap), giving a 520×200 mi rectangle bounded by radius-r circles at the endpoints

Probability of Containment P(C): a Gaussian (normal) distribution across the y-axis (perpendicular to flight path), centered on the flight path, spanning 3 standard deviations, justified from crash-cause statistics — simplified to be uniform along the x-axis (flight-path direction)
Classical Search Theory decomposition: total find probability P = P(C)·P(D), with P(C) from the spatial distribution and P(D) from sensor capability

Monte Carlo simulation engine: 5,000 simulated crashes drawn from the normal distribution; search terminates when |S_i − ⟨x_c, y_c⟩| < d (sight radius). 100–500 trials per candidate path, recording mean/median/SD/SEM/95% CI

Six candidate flight-path policies evaluated under perfect detection: (1) completely random unit-vector walk, (2) bounded random with orthogonal redirection at box edges, (3) bounded WEIGHTED random (15:1 random-to-toward-endpoint bias), (4) descending striped (boustrophedon/lawnmower, step 2d to avoid overlap), (5) spiral striped (lawnmower seeded at the high-probability center, expanding outward via C(u)=2(−1)^u·u), (6) sinusoidal path (amplitude=d, wavelength=2d) for wider per-pass coverage

Bayesian Search Theory (the winning model): after each unsuccessful pass, posterior update P'(C)=P(C)(1−P(D_u))/(1−P(C)P(D_u)) for the searched cell and P'(C_i)=P(C_i)/(1−P(C)P(D_u)) for unsearched cells; greedy next-pass selection always targets the current maximum-probability stripe

Detection-probability sub-model: P(D_u)=P(R)·P(O|R), with radar detection P(R)≈80% (from AN/APS-119 test data) and operator identification P(O)≈97%→~1 (90 s dwell time from sight-crossing kinematics), yielding P(D_u)≈0.8
Sensitivity/stability tests: re-running the Bayesian path for a much smaller Cessna 172 (P(D) scaled to ~2%) and a two-plane center-out striped scenario
Human cold-water survival model: ~18 h hypothermia death window at ~20°C Atlantic used as the success threshold that the search-time CI must beat
key_techniques: Progressive model laddering — six paths under an idealized assumption first, then re-derive under realistic imperfect detection, so every added complexity is motivated by a demonstrated shortcoming of the prior model

Statistical rigor for ranking candidates: non-overlapping 95% confidence intervals used as the formal test of whether one search path is significantly better than another (SEM = σ/√n, CI = [μ−2·SE, μ+2·SE])

Deliberate handling of skewed distributions: explicitly comparing mean vs median to diagnose right-skew, then switching to MEDIAN as the comparison statistic when distributions are heavily skewed (this is what flips the recommendation toward the Bayesian path)

Decoupling 'time-to-find' from 'probability-of-finding': recognizing that a path with lower mean time (spiral, 80% found) is worse than one with higher mean time (Bayesian, 95% found) because unsuccessful searches are censored out of the mean
Variable time-step tuning per solution (15 min for unbounded random down to 0.001 h for deterministic paths) — coarser steps where paths rarely terminate, finer where computation is cheap
Greedy maximum-probability-first heuristic to make the intractable Bayesian optimal-search-path computable
Grounding every parameter in cited real-world data: glide ratios, P-8/P-3 radar specs, FAA 30-min comms rule, AN/APS-119 detection test, operator visual-search study, cold-water survival tables
Distribution simplification with stated justification: collapsing a genuinely complex 2D containment distribution into a 1D normal in y, uniform in x, with an explicit defense of the approximation
why_it_won: From a judge's perspective this paper wins on three axes that O-award papers consistently nail. First, COMPLETE PROBLEM DECOMPOSITION: it solves both halves (where to search AND how to search) rather than just one, and connects them through Search Theory's P=P(C)·P(D). Second, a CLEAR, HONEST MODEL LADDER with a defensible final choice: six paths are benchmarked, the best (spiral) is identified, then the assumption that makes it look good (perfect detection) is removed, and the recommendation is correctly revised to the Bayesian path on the basis of the right metric (95% found and equal medians, not the misleadingly-low censored mean). That intellectual move — refusing to pick the lower-mean solution because the mean is biased by censoring — is exactly the kind of statistical maturity judges reward. Third, REAL-WORLD ANCHORING AND CONSEQUENCE: every constant is cited from real specs and the success criterion is a concrete human stake (the 18-hour hypothermia window), so the recommended ~6 h mean search time has obvious meaning. Add disciplined statistical inference (CIs to prove significance), sensitivity testing (Cessna, two planes), strong scaffolding (variable table, layered assumptions, per-solution template), and a polished MH370-framed narrative plus the required press-conference document, and the paper reads as both technically sound and decision-ready.
section_highlights: Structure is a major strength. The paper uses a rigorous per-solution template repeated identically across all 6+ paths: Brief Description → How It Works (with governing vector equation) → diagram → example trial → simulation data (n, median, mean, SD, SE, 95% CI) → histogram → conclusion comparing CIs to prior solutions. This makes eight competing models effortless to compare and signals control of the analysis. Front matter is exemplary: a complete two-table variable glossary (lost-plane vs search variables with symbols and units), assumptions partitioned into Environmental / Crash / Search, and model-specific assumptions stated locally at each model. The summary sheet is concrete and quantitative (38 h random → 3.6 h systematic → 4.7 h Bayesian, 95% within a day), telling the whole arc up front. The Results section consolidates all CIs into comparison charts (random vs nonrandom split onto separate axes because of scale), and the Conclusion explicitly weighs strengths/weaknesses and limiting assumptions. The narrative framing (Amelia Earhart → MH370) and the cold-water-survival deadline give the math human stakes, and the required press-conference document is written in plausible airline-PR voice.
pitfalls_or_limitations: The 24-hour computational cap on the Bayesian simulation censors long searches; unsuccessful trials are dropped from the mean, biasing it downward and forcing the awkward (though correctly-handled) median comparison. The team flags this but it weakens the headline numbers

The probability distribution is heavily simplified: a genuinely 2D containment field is collapsed to 1D normal in y and uniform in x, and they admit the standard deviation 'has a high degree of uncertainty' — the entire P(C) backbone rests on an estimated, uncited σ

Flat-Earth / linear-flight-path assumption and total neglect of wind, ocean currents, debris drift, weather, and sea state — currents in particular are central to real MH370-type searches and their omission limits realism

The Bayesian 'optimal' path uses a greedy maximum-probability-first heuristic; it is not proven optimal and the team concedes residual overlap on pass transitions, so it doesn't fully realize Bayesian search's theoretical advantage

Detection is treated as a single-step success (finding debris = finding the plane) with spatially-uniform 80% P(D) inside the radar radius — real radar detection falls off with range and debris may not exist or may be ambiguous
Several inputs are stand-ins: classified P-8 sensor performance is approximated by a 1979 AN/APS-119 report, and P(O)≈1 leans on a single 1960 visual-search study
Mobilization/transit time to the search area is assumed negligible and zeroed out, which understates real time-to-rescue against the 18-hour survival deadline
Some figures appear OCR-garbled/placeholder in this corpus copy (e.g., several scatter/line tables), so a few quantitative plots can't be fully verified from the markdown
reusable_patterns: The 'idealized-then-realistic' model ladder: solve under a simplifying assumption (perfect detection), rank candidates, then remove the assumption and re-rank — each layer is justified by a concrete defect of the previous one

Per-candidate evaluation template (description → mechanism+equation → diagram → example → stats table → histogram → CI comparison) as a repeatable scaffold whenever you must compare many strategies/algorithms
Use non-overlapping 95% CIs as the explicit decision rule for 'is method A significantly better than B' — a clean, defensible way to rank Monte Carlo results
Diagnose skew via mean-vs-median gap and switch to the median (or report both) when distributions are right-skewed; never rank skewed/censored simulations by mean alone
Separate the two success metrics — expected time AND probability of success — and recommend on whichever matters for the stakeholder objective, especially when censoring biases the time metric
Search Theory framing P = P(C)·P(D) decomposing a search/detection problem into a spatial containment prior and a sensor detection model — transplantable to any coverage/search/inspection problem
Bayesian posterior coverage update for sequential search (down-weight searched cells by (1−P(D)), renormalize, greedily re-target the new max) — a reusable recipe for any 'search-and-update' task
Tie the optimization target to a real consequence threshold (here the 18-h cold-water survival window) so that 'good enough' is defined by the domain, not arbitrarily

Ground every model constant in a cited real-world source and tabulate them, then run sensitivity/stability checks by swapping the key parameter (smaller target, extra searcher) to demonstrate robustness and flexibility
Variable-resolution time-stepping in simulation: coarse steps for expensive/rarely-terminating cases, fine steps for cheap deterministic ones
